title: C3: Residue contract spec (the safe-mowing gate)
description: Define exactly what a closing epic must link before it can close: >=1 assertion-register entry (A*/I*/E*) or an architecture-doc edit. This gate is what makes deleting settled paradocs lossless. Single most important rule in the transformation.
design:
RESIDUE CONTRACT v0 — the gate that makes deleting a settled paradoc lossless.

PRINCIPLE (doctrine): "Partial work is allowed; lost learning is not."
This operationalizes the Parallax `learning state: retained/promoted` field as a
hard precondition on epic close + projection deletion.

TWO GATES before an epic may close:
  G1 (descent): V=0 — every child conjecture closed with a typed verdict, OR
      remaining children explicitly superseded/handed-off (edges named).
  G2 (residue): >=1 durable residue link that RESOLVES (see allowed sinks).

ALLOWED RESIDUE SINKS (priority order):
  1. assertion-register entry (A*/I*/E* in conjecture-assertion-ledger)  <- canonical harvest
  2. architecture/doctrine doc edit (SHA touching current-architecture.md, runtime-invariants.md, doctrine)
  3. incident/evidence doc (REQUIRED for falsified/weakened — the negative result)
  4. merged code commit/PR when the code itself IS the durable artifact

REQUIRED RESIDUE BY CLOSE TYPE:
  settled(supported):   sink #1 or #2 (or #4 if code is the artifact)
  falsified / weakened: sink #3 mandatory (Problem-Documentation-First; negative learning is most-lost)
  superseded:           successor epic linked via supersedes/discovered-from; open edges inherited
  blocked/open_handoff: NOT a close — epic stays open; open edges named

NOT VALID RESIDUE (anti-laundering):
  - the paradoc/projection itself (it is the scaffold being mowed; cannot be its own residue)
  - a ledger entry alone (process record != promoted learning)
  - "see the bead" / circular self-reference

MOWABILITY PREDICATE (what doccheck computes once C2 reader lands):
  a docs/mission-*.md projection is safe-to-delete IFF
    epic.status == closed  AND  all residue links resolve.
  doccheck emits a "mowable" list -> deletion becomes mechanical, not a judgment call.

ENFORCEMENT PATH: new doccheck rule R-residue, report-only first (per docs-truth philosophy),
graduates to a hard gate in C10.

OPEN POLICY CHOICES (need owner ratification — these are doctrine, not mechanics):
  Q1: Is sink #4 (merged code alone) sufficient for a SUPPORTED settle, or must every
      supported settle also leave a #1/#2 written-knowledge trace?
  Q2: For superseded epics, is a lineage edge alone enough, or must the successor also
      carry forward the predecessor's residue obligation until IT settles?
notes:
RATIFIED Q1 (owner): SUPPORTED settle accepts code-as-residue ONLY when the PR/commit is auto-linked to an assertion-register entry (>=1 line). Pure code with no assertion anchor does NOT close. Updated sink #4 accordingly.
PROPOSED Q2 (recommended, pending confirm): SUPERSEDED carries forward the predecessor's residue obligation to the successor (defer-but-never-escape). Escape valve: a genuine reframe discharges the inherited debt with a single assertion line naming why the predecessor frame was abandoned. doccheck R-residue walks the supersedes chain at close.
RATIFIED Q2 (owner): carry-forward confirmed. SUPERSEDED transfers residue obligation to successor (defer-but-never-escape); reframe discharges via single assertion line. Residue Contract v0 COMPLETE.
close_reason: supported: Residue Contract v0 ratified (Q1 code-needs-assertion-anchor; Q2 carry-forward). Deliverable = the contract in design notes; self-residue is the contract itself. Promote to doctrine in C10. Enables C9 mowability predicate.

title: C1: Beads capability spike
description: Confirm beads supports the mapping: custom/labelled types (mission,conjecture,heresy), close-reasons or notes for verdicts (supported/weakened/falsified/superseded/discovered), append-only event log for the ledger, and clean merge under 12-way concurrent orchestration. Output: a can/cant sheet; flag any column needing a label-encoding fallback.
design:
CAN/CANT SHEET (spike findings):
[CAN] Custom types: -t conjecture accepted at create (label 'conjecture' also applied as belt-and-suspenders).
[CAN] Verdicts: close --reason carries free-text verdict (supported/weakened/falsified/superseded); pair with a label for queryability. 'discovered' = new sub-issue via discovered-from edge.
[CAN] Ledger: THREE encodings — --append-notes (in-place, simplest), comments (comment_count field), or first-class event beads (--type=event, queryable). Dolt history adds free per-write provenance.
[CAN] V-as-query: parent/child + blocked-by edges native; bd ready already computes the frontier (only C1 was ready).
[CAN] Sharing/merge: storage = embedded Dolt (cell-level merge + branching); .beads/issues.jsonl is the git-committed interchange (now auto-exported). 12-way concurrent merge is Dolt's design point but UNVERIFIED under load -> defer the real stress test to C8.
[FALLBACK] none required; no column needed label-only encoding.
VERDICT: supported.
notes: PASS 1 / Move: probe / Verdict: supported — custom types accepted; jsonl export live (11 issues); close-reason carries verdict text; event beads + dolt history available for ledger. ΔV expected -1.
close_reason: supported: beads natively supports every column of the mapping (types, verdicts, ledger, V-as-query, Dolt merge). No fallbacks needed. Open edge: 12-way merge-under-load deferred to C8.

112
@@MG-PREDEP
# Red class bug: users get logged out during staging deploys. Root cause:
# requestSessionState() in frontend/src/lib/auth.js throws on ANY non-2xx
# with no retry. checkSession() in App.svelte catches all errors and
# transitions to signed_out. During deploy, auth service has 2-14s downtime;
# any /auth/session call in that window triggers permanent logout.
# Fix: distinguish transient (5xx, network) from permanent (401/403). Add
# retry with exponential backoff for transient. Only sign out on explicit
# {authenticated: false}.
# Heresy delta: discovered + repaired.
# Commits: 7c2b0259 (docs), 6286f89f (fix).
@@MG-END

title: Mission: Host Sandbox Deletion v0
description: Delete the host-side go-choir-sandbox service (sandbox-m1) on node-b. It's a remnant from the desktop bootstrap before Apple containerization. The platform computer should be a real VM (or container machine), not a bare host process. Today: nix/node-b.nix defines go-choir-sandbox as a host systemd service with SANDBOX_ID=sandbox-m1. The proxy routes to it when no VM is running. Sourcecycled shares its runtime store path. The fix: the platform computer becomes a real VM, the host sandbox service is deleted, the proxy routes via vmctl, sourcecycled dispatches via HTTP. This unblocks the sourcecycled SQLite consolidation.

@@MG-HEAD
# === Naming Rectification + Host Sandbox Deletion (2026-06-29) ===
# The naming rectification plan (docs/naming-rectification-2026-06-27.md)
# audits all object/concept/process/system names. The key renames:
#   sandbox → autoputer (service/binary name)
#   platformd → corpusd (service/binary name)
#   sourcegraph → fold into cycle (delete package)
#   channel → mailbox (actor protocol)
# Plus: delete the host-side sandbox-m1 service on node-b. It's a remnant
# from the desktop bootstrap before Apple containerization. The platform
# computer should be a real VM (or container machine), not a bare host
# process.
@@MG-PREDEP
# Delete the host-side go-choir-sandbox service (sandbox-m1) on node-b.
# This is a remnant from the desktop bootstrap before Apple containerization.
# The platform computer should be a real VM (or container machine), not a
# bare host process running directly on the node-b host.
#
# Today: nix/node-b.nix defines go-choir-sandbox as a host systemd service
# with SANDBOX_ID=sandbox-m1, RUNTIME_STORE_PATH=/var/lib/go-choir/runtime/
# runtime.db. The proxy routes to it (PROXY_SANDBOX_URL=http://127.0.0.1:8085)
# when no VM is running. Sourcecycled shares its runtime store path. The
# host sandbox runs the Universal Wire pipeline (processor agents, texture
# agents, publication).
#
# The fix: the platform computer becomes a real VM (like user computers).
# The host sandbox service is deleted. The proxy routes to the platform VM
# via vmctl. Sourcecycled dispatches to the platform VM's sandbox endpoint.
# The Nix assertion about shared store paths is deleted — sourcecycled
# dispatches via HTTP, not via shared filesystem.
#
# This unblocks the sourcecycled SQLite consolidation: without a host
# sandbox sharing the filesystem, sourcecycled can write to Dolt directly
# (or to its own Dolt store that syncs with the platform VM's Dolt).
#
# Dependency: the platform VM must exist and run the Universal Wire
# pipeline before the host sandbox can be deleted. This is a VM lifecycle
# change — vmctl boots a platform VM at startup, the proxy routes to it.
@@MG-END

# The Choir web desktop as an MCP App (SEP-1865, ratified January 2026).
# The MCP server declares ui:// resources alongside its tools. When an agent
# calls choir_show_missions, the host (Claude Desktop, ChatGPT, VS Code
# Copilot, Microsoft 365 Copilot) renders the kanban board inline in the
# conversation. When an agent calls choir_read_wire, the host renders the
# wire reader inline. The user interacts with the UI inside Claude's
# conversation — drag cards, read articles, trigger cycles — without
# switching tabs. The iframe calls back to the Choir MCP server via JSON-RPC
# over postMessage through the host.
#
# MCP Apps use ui:// URI scheme, mimeType text/html;profile=mcp-app, sandboxed
# iframe rendering, JSON-RPC over postMessage. SDK
# (@modelcontextprotocol/ext-apps) has Svelte examples — Choir's frontend is
# already Svelte. The convert-web-app skill turns an existing web app into a
# hybrid web + MCP App. Choir's web desktop is the existing web app.
#
# This is the killer entree into choir-in-choir: the human and the agent
# collaborate in the same conversation. The human says "show me the mission
# board," Claude calls the tool, the kanban renders inline, the human drags
# a card, the agent sees the change, the agent works, the board updates in
# real time. No tab switching. No separate app.
@@MG-END

beads-mission-state-v0,agent-api-graph-native-v0
@@MG-PREDEP
# A kanban board inside Choir that visualizes beads mission state. Columns:
# backlog → ready → in_progress → review → closed. Cards are conjectures
# (sub-issues); swimlanes are epics (missions). Drag to advance state. Click
# a card to see the conjecture's move-log (bead notes), verdict, and residue
# links. This is the human-facing entree into choir-in-choir: a human can see
# the mission DAG, watch agents work in real time, and advance or redirect
# conjectures visually — without `bd` or the CLI.
#
# Four entry points into choir-in-choir, all hitting the same graph-native
# API (agent-api-graph-native-v0):
#   1. Beads kanban UI (human, visual) — this mission
#   2. Raw API (agent, HTTP) — /api/v1/ endpoints
#   3. CLI (agent, shell) — `choir` commands wrapping the API (nucleus-cli-v0)
#   4. MCP server (agent, no shell) — tools for web-UI agents (agent-api-mcp-server-v0)
# The difference is presentation, not data model. The kanban UI is the
# Svelte frontend calling /api/v1/ with session cookie auth. The CLI is Go
# calling /api/v1/ with API key auth. The MCP server is a thin wrapper
# exposing the same endpoints as tools.
#
# Implementation: the kanban UI is a Svelte route in the existing frontend
# (cmd/frontend or the Wails desktop app). It calls /api/v1/missions (new
# endpoint that reads from .beads/) and /api/v1/missions/:id/conjectures.
# State transitions are PATCH /api/v1/conjectures/:id with the new status.
# The beads store (.beads/issues.jsonl) is the source of truth; the UI is a
# projection. Real-time updates via SSE or polling.
@@MG-END

description:

design:
@@MG-IDX
103
@@MG-DEPORD
nucleus-cli-v0,nucleus-capsule-runtime-v0,mutation-transaction-coordinator-v0,agent-api-mcp-server-v0
@@MG-PREDEP
# The full choir-in-choir loop: an agent in a persistent computer uses the
# Choir CLI to (1) read the current state of its computer (textures, graph,
# runs), (2) spawn a candidate computer fork, (3) run capsules inside the
# candidate for risky experiments, (4) verify the results in read-only
# verifier capsules, (5) produce a promotion certificate, (6) promote the
# candidate to active via mutation transaction, (7) publish the result as a
# typed artifact (AppChangePackage or texture). This is the vision's "Choir
# developing Choir inside Choir" — the agent is both user and developer of
# the system. The loop is: read → fork → experiment → verify → promote →
# publish. The agent uses the MCP server for read/search/traverse, the CLI
# for VM/computer/capsule lifecycle, and the mutation transaction coordinator
# for promotion. This mission wires the loop together end-to-end. Proof: an
# agent can make a reviewable change to Choir, inside Choir, without a
# browser, and the change goes through the same promotion path a human would
# use.
@@MG-END

description:

@@MG-IDX
101
@@MG-DEPORD
nucleus-cli-v0
@@MG-PREDEP
# Implement Nucleus-style capsules as an execution primitive inside user,
# candidate, and platform computers. A capsule is an ephemeral, effect-fenced
# sandbox used by a persistent computer to run risky, parallel, or disposable
# work. The handoff doc (2026-06-10) specifies the full architecture: strict-
# agent mode, network deny/allowlist, copy-in-out or bind-ro workspace, tmpfs
# home, pinned Nix-built toolchain rootfs, no secrets by default, typed output
# directory. The effect report schema captures downloads, filesystem writes,
# package manager attempts, service changes, network attempts. Capsules are
# the "disposable effect chamber" in the three-layer model: agent lives in
# persistent computer, risky effects run in capsules, effects become durable
# through mutation transactions. This mission implements the capsule runtime
# inside a VM (not a separate VM — a sandbox inside the VM, lighter than a
# Firecracker microVM). Candidate: NixOS containers, systemd-nspawn, or
# Nucleus itself if it matures. The capsule is to the VM what the VM is to
# the host — one level down in the isolation hierarchy.
@@MG-END

@@MG-PREDEP
# Implement the durable MutationTransaction object from the handoff doc. This
# is the saga-like protocol that coordinates base refs, staged effects,
# verification, commit, and rollback across all ledgers (VM/runtime, Dolt/app,
# source/build, blob/content, artifact/provenance, route identity). Phases:
# Begin → Stage → Execute → Capture → Classify → Verify → Commit → Rollback.
# Not one ACID transaction — a saga with strong local transactions (Dolt
# commit, git patch, NixOS generation, route pointer switch) and explicit
# cross-ledger coordination. The promotion certificate proves: no foreground
# update silently lost, no candidate mutation touched canonical state before
# promotion, typed deltas merged or explicitly conflicted, verifier contracts
# ran, route switch has rollback target, retry is idempotent. This is the
# cathedral that coordinates the existing Lego blocks (NixOS generations,
# Dolt branches, git worktrees, Qdrant shadow collections, route pointers).
@@MG-END

# === Nucleus / Choir-In-Choir Control Plane (2026-06-29) ===
# The vision: a coding agent (Devin, Claude Code, Codex) uses a Choir CLI to
# control one or more VMs which run many computers. The agent is the
# supervisory agent living in a persistent computer. The CLI is its hands.
# Capsules are disposable effect chambers for risky/parallel work. Candidate
# computers are speculative futures. Promotion is the transaction that turns
# selected effects into canonical state. This is choir-in-choir: Choir
# developing Choir inside Choir, agents using Choir as a tool to manage Choir
# computers.
@@MG-PREDEP
# Build a `choir` CLI that agents use to control VMs and computers. Today
# there is no choir CLI — only service binaries (vmctl, sandbox, proxy,
# sourcecycled, maild, etc.) and the browser/frontend. The CLI is the agent's
# control surface: `choir computer list`, `choir computer create`,
# `choir computer shell`, `choir vm boot`, `choir vm stop`, `choir capsule run`,
# `choir cycle trigger`, `choir texture read`, `choir texture write`,
# `choir search`, `choir graph traverse`. It wraps the agent API
# (agent-api-graph-native-v0) for read/write and vmctl's HTTP API for VM
# lifecycle. Auth via API key (Bearer token). The CLI is what makes
# choir-in-choir real: an agent in a persistent computer uses the CLI to
# spawn candidate computers, run capsules, trigger cycles, and manage the
# fleet — without a browser.
@@MG-END

design:
@@MG-IDX
96
@@MG-DEPORD
desktop-embedded-dolt-cloud-v0,nucleus-capsule-runtime-v0
@@MG-PREDEP
# The Mac (or other OS) Wails desktop app gets a real VM with nucleus capsules
# in local mode. Today local mode launches sandbox/gateway/proxy as child
# processes (host-process mode, no isolation). The vision is a real VM running
# on the desktop with the same capsule runtime as the platform VMs. The agent
# in the desktop VM can spawn capsules for risky work, just like on the
# platform. This unifies the desktop and platform execution models — the same
# code runs in both, the difference is scale and location.
#
# Primary substrate: Apple `container` (github.com/apple/container). It has
# two modes that map directly to Choir's architecture:
#   - `container machine` = persistent Linux VM (the desktop local VM)
#   - `container run` = ephemeral lightweight VMs (the capsules)
# Capsules are sibling VMs on the macOS host, not nested sandboxes inside the
# container machine. This is stronger isolation than systemd-nspawn and
# simpler than nesting. `container` uses the Containerization Swift package
# (successor to vfkit, which was used in a previous prototype). OCI images
# built by Nix, distributed via registry or bundled in the Wails app.
# Requires Apple Silicon + macOS 26.
#
# Fallback substrates (pursue as needed for stability, security, performance,
# and partnerships):
#   - vfkit (Go bindings to Virtualization.framework, used in prior prototype)
#   - Lima/colima (QEMU-based, mature on macOS, works on older macOS)
#   - Firecracker on macOS (not available today, Linux/KVM only)
#   - cloud-hypervisor (Rust-based, cross-platform, immature on macOS)
#   - Host-process (current fallback, no isolation, no capsules)
#
# Key constraint: the desktop VM must not require a full NixOS install on
# the host. The VM image is an OCI image built by Nix and distributed via
# registry or bundled. The host only needs `container` + the Wails app.
@@MG-END

# === Dolt Unification Spine (2026-06-29) ===
# The texture store is already on embedded Dolt. The object graph has a Dolt
# backend (tested but not primary). The Base journal is on SQLite, reinventing
# what Dolt does natively (hash chain, cursor, conflict detection). This spine
# moves everything to Dolt and makes sync native.

# (Append → DOLT_COMMIT, Cursor → remote tracking branch, VerifyChain → Dolt

@@MG-END

# choir.texture.edition, choir.texture.autopaper, choir.texture.algorithm,
# choir.texture.styleguide, choir.texture.schedule, choir.texture.renderer.
# Register the "transcludes" edge kind — the core morphism. Create shadow
# nodes for existing textures (version_id points to current revision_id).
# Create edges for existing Wire.texture transclusion refs (parsed from the
# current edition content). This is the type theory step: textures become
# first-class object graph nodes, transclusions become first-class edges.
@@MG-END

title: Mission: Object Graph Dolt Backend In Production v0
description:
Mission: Mission: Object Graph Dolt Backend In Production v0
Mission node: objectgraph-dolt-production-v0 (kind=spine, status=planned)
Paradoc: docs/mission-spine-object-graph-ubiquitous-v0.md
Sources:

  - internal/runtime/objectgraph_runtime.go
  - docs/overnight-pr-review-verdicts-2026-06-29.md

Imported from docs/mission-graph.yaml (shadow mode, reversible).
design:
@@MG-IDX
83
@@MG-HEAD
# === Type Theory / Object Graph Ubiquitous Spine (2026-06-29) ===
# These missions implement the vision's core architectural shift: textures are
# object graph nodes, transclusions are edges, the wire API is graph traversal.
# The object graph already exists with a Dolt backend (tested but not wired as
# primary in production). The texture store is already on embedded Dolt. The
# web capture path already uses graph traversal. The article path does not —
# it uses a singleton Wire.texture document with regex-parsed text references.
# This spine makes everything graph-native and deletes the singleton.
@@MG-PREDEP
# The Dolt backend (internal/objectgraph/dolt_store.go) exists and is tested
# (dolt_branch_test.go proves DOLT_BRANCH/CHECKOUT/COMMIT/MERGE work on the
# object graph schema). The runtime's newRuntimeObjectGraphService already
# creates a DoltStore — but the production object graph on the platform VM
# still has a 538M SQLite sidecar (state.objectgraph.db) from sourcecycled.
# This mission wires the Dolt backend as the sole primary in production and
# migrates any existing SQLite data into Dolt.
@@MG-END

title: C4: Status/verdict vocab mapping frozen
description: Freeze Choir<->beads mapping: planned/working/open_handoff/settled/superseded/blocked -> open/in_progress/blocked/closed + close-reason + labels; conjecture verdicts -> sub-issue close-reasons; discovered -> new sub-issue via discovered-from edge; V = count(open conjecture sub-issues).
design:
VOCAB FREEZE v0 — canonical Choir<->beads mapping. doccheck and the Parallax skill bind to THIS.

MISSION STATUS (epic):
  planned       -> open (no assignee)
  working       -> in_progress
  blocked       -> open + label 'blocked'  (reserve native 'blocked' status for hard tool-level blocks)
  open_handoff  -> in_progress + label 'handoff' + reassign owner
  settled       -> closed, reason prefix "settled: ..."
  superseded    -> closed, reason prefix "superseded: ...", + supersedes edge to successor

CONJECTURE VERDICT (sub-issue close): reason prefix is the verdict, lowercase:
  supported | weakened | falsified | superseded
  discovered -> NOT a close; create a NEW sub-issue with a discovered-from edge (raises V)

KIND (epic label):           spine | side | docs_truth | evidence | superseded
MUTATION CLASS (label):      mc:green | mc:yellow | mc:orange | mc:red | mc:black
HERESY DELTA (label):        heresy:discovered | heresy:introduced | heresy:repaired

VARIANT V:   count(children of epic where status != closed). Derived, never hand-typed.
            (C2 reader implements beadsVariant() as the canonical computation.)

WITNESS / RECEIPT:  "Receipt:" lines in notes for trace/artifact refs; --external-ref for commit SHA / PR / gh-N.
BUDGET:             label 'budget:granted=N' / tracked in notes; solvency = a query, not a field.

PARSE CONTRACT for doccheck (so labels are machine-readable):
  - prefixed labels use 'key:value' (mc:, heresy:, budget:); bare labels are sets (spine, conjecture).
  - close-reason verdict = first whitespace-delimited token before ':'.

DECISIONS:
  - Rich Choir vocab is preserved as close-reason-prefix + labels (no information loss vs the old YAML enum).
  - Native beads 'blocked' status reserved for tool/dependency blocks; mission 'blocked' is a label so it
    coexists with in_progress work. Revisit in C7 if it causes ambiguity.
close_reason: supported: vocab freeze v0 complete; full Choir<->beads mapping with no enum information loss. Binds C2 reader (label parse contract) and C5 import (status mapping). Promote to doctrine in C10.
